stem: Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{e^{[[a]]x} - 1}{[[b]]x} \), an indeterminate form of type \( \tfrac{0}{0} \).
solution: Both numerator and denominator vanish at \( x = 0 \). By L'Hopital's rule, \( \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{[[a]]e^{[[a]]x}}{[[b]]} = \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the coefficients.
This multiplies the coefficients instead of dividing.
This cancels the coefficients entirely, leaving 1.
This has the wrong sign.
This keeps only the numerator's coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{[[a]]^{x} - [[b]]^{x}}{x} \), an indeterminate form of type \( \tfrac{0}{0} \).
solution: Both numerator and denominator vanish at \( x = 0 \), since \( [[a]]^{0} - [[b]]^{0} = 0 \). Differentiating top and bottom, \( \frac{d}{dx}\left([[a]]^{x} - [[b]]^{x}\right) = [[a]]^{x}\ln [[a]] - [[b]]^{x}\ln [[b]] \) and the denominator's derivative is \( 1 \). Evaluating at \( x = 0 \) gives \( \ln [[a]] - \ln [[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the logarithms instead of subtracting them.
This has the subtraction in the wrong order.
This drops the logarithm entirely, using the bases directly.
This is the ratio of the bases, not the difference of their logarithms.
This takes the log of the product instead of the difference of the logs.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1 - \cos([[a]]x)}{([[b]]x)^{2}} \), an indeterminate form of type \( \tfrac{0}{0} \) that requires applying L'Hopital's rule twice.
solution: Both numerator and denominator vanish at \( x = 0 \). Applying L'Hopital's rule once gives \( \frac{[[a]]\sin([[a]]x)}{2[[b]]^{2}x} \), still of type \( \tfrac{0}{0} \); applying it again gives \( \frac{[[a]]^{2}\cos([[a]]x)}{2[[b]]^{2}} \), which at \( x = 0 \) equals \( \frac{[[a]]^{2}}{2[[b]]^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square the coefficients (stopping after only one differentiation).
This multiplies the coefficients instead of using the squared-over-twice-squared form.
This forgets the factor of 2 in the denominator.
This forgets to square the numerator's coefficient.
This has the wrong sign; \( \cos(0) = 1 > 0 \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\ln(1 + [[a]]x)}{[[b]]x} \), an indeterminate form of type \( \tfrac{0}{0} \).
solution: Both numerator and denominator vanish at \( x = 0 \), since \( \ln(1) = 0 \). By L'Hopital's rule, differentiate to get \( \frac{[[a]]/(1+[[a]]x)}{[[b]]} \), which at \( x = 0 \) equals \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the coefficients.
This multiplies the coefficients instead of dividing.
This cancels the coefficients entirely, leaving 1.
This has the wrong sign.
This keeps only the numerator's coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{[[a]]x + \ln x}{[[b]]x} \), an indeterminate form of type \( \tfrac{\infty}{\infty} \).
solution: Both numerator and denominator grow without bound. By L'Hopital's rule, differentiate top and bottom: \( \frac{[[a]] + 1/x}{[[b]]} \to \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \) as \( x \to \infty \) (the \( \ln x \) term grows too slowly to affect the leading behavior).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the coefficients.
This multiplies the coefficients instead of dividing.
This wrongly assumes the logarithm dominates, forcing the ratio to 0.
This assumes numerator and denominator grow at the same rate exactly.
This adds the coefficients instead of dividing them.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
